turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court this morning in case 16-1150, Hall versus Hall. The Federal Courts of Appeals have jurisdiction over "appeals" from all final decisions of the district courts. Under normal circumstances when a district court issues a final decision in a case, in other words when it finally decides the issues on the merits, the losing party may immediately appeal. Here we are asked to decide what happens when two or more cases originally filed as separate actions are consolidated by a federal district court and one of the cases is decided before the others are complete. May the losing party appeal from the decision resolving the first case while the other cases continue in the district court, or must that party wait to appeal until all of the consolidated cases are finally decided? We answer this question in the context of two cases pitting a brother Samuel against his sister Elsa over the handling of their mother's finances. Samuel sued Elsa in one case. Elsa sued Samuel in the other. The issues in the two cases were not exactly the same, but there was a lot of overlap. To save everyone time and money, the federal district court consolidated the two cases. That meant, for example, that evidence in one case could be used in the other, that hearings would address issues in both cases, and most importantly, that there would be one trial before one jury to decide the facts. Now, when it was all over, Samuel won both cases, but the district court granted Elsa a new trial in one case and before that new trial could take place, Elsa appealed in the other case. The Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit threw out Elsa's appeal as premature, reasoning that the two cases had merged into one when they were consolidated and that there could be no final decision and thus no right to appeal until both cases were fully resolved. We have to decide whether that is right and to do so we look to where the authority to consolidate cases comes from. That is Rule 42 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure. The rule provides that if "actions before the Court involve a common question of law or fact, the Court may consolidate the actions." Well, that does not tell us very much. The term consolidate as used in the rule is ambiguous. The word sometimes means that discrete units have merged completely, as when a company consolidates two branches. Other times it does not signal a complete merger, when we say she consolidated her books, no one thinks that the books became one book. But the history of Rule 42 tells us how to interpret the term consolidate. That is because Rule 42 was modeled on a much older statute, enacted by Congress in 1813, and for a 125 years the federal courts interpreting that statute almost always understood the word "consolidate" to mean that when multiple cases are joined together, they do not merge into one. For example, just five years before Rule 42 became law, we decided an important case called Johnson versus Manhattan Railway Co., and in Johnson the district court attempted to use the 1813 statute to turn two separate cases into one. We rejected that attempt. As we explained, this is a quote, "Consolidation does not merge the suits into a single cause, or change the rights of the parties, or make those who are parties in one suit parties in another." In our view that understanding of consolidate did not change when Rule 42 became effective in 1938. Like the 1813 statute, Rule 42 gave the district courts the authority to consolidate cases, but Rule 42 did not define the term and for that reason we think it meant just what it meant under the 1813 statute. As Justice Felix Frankfurter once put it, "if a word is obviously transplanted from another legal source, whether the common law or other legislation, it brings the old soil with it." Here the drafters of Rule 42 transplanted the term consolidate from the 1813 statute into the new rule. The rule therefore took the old soil with it, including the 125 years of decisions, especially from this Court, holding that when two or more cases are consolidated, they do not merge into one, a decision that fully resolves one of the consolidated cases, even if the other cases are far from over is a final decision from which the losing party may immediately seek review in the Court of Appeals. Now, Samuel says that this understanding of the rule is going to cause all sorts of practical problems. We do not think we can decide the case based on our view of what would be the best policy given how we read the rule, but we do have authority to propose new rules. Federal Rules Advisory Committee's very impressive groups of lawyers, professors, and judges help us consider whether to do so and if the problems of the sort Samuel foresees do come up, they can be raised before the appropriate Rules Committee and so we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit. Our opinion is unanimous.
